resources.string: Take a clear photo of a single leaf for the most accurate result.
Use bright, even light and avoid harsh shadows.
Capture a single leaf; keep the background simple.
Hold steady so the leaf is in sharp focus.
Fill most of the frame with the leaf.
Models did not strongly agree. Try another photo.
Models show good agreement for this result.
Higher means the models agreed more strongly.
Leafivy helps identify apple leaf diseases using on-device machine learning. Images are processed locally on your phone.
Your photos are not uploaded to any server by this app. No account is required. You stay in control of your data.
Make sure the apple leaf is clearly visible and not blurry before processing. Press Process Image to start the analysis.
Preview of the apple leaf image to be analyzed
Frog eye leaf spot is a fungal disease caused by Botryosphaeria obtusa. It thrives in warm, wet conditions and can reduce photosynthesis significantly.
Remove and destroy infected leaves immediately to reduce spore spread.
Apply a labeled fungicide such as captan or thiophanate-methyl.
Avoid overhead irrigation to keep foliage dry.
Prune trees to improve airflow and reduce humidity within the canopy.
Rake and dispose of fallen leaves to remove overwintering spores.
Use resistant apple varieties when replanting.
Recheck leaves every 5-7 days during wet season.
Repeat fungicide application as per label directions if symptoms persist.
Powdery mildew coats young apple leaves and shoots with a white powdery film. It stunts growth and weakens the tree over time.
Prune and remove visibly infected shoots and leaves.
Apply a sulfur-based or systemic fungicide approved for powdery mildew.
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization which promotes susceptible soft growth.
Choose mildew-resistant apple varieties when possible.
Maintain good airflow by pruning the canopy annually.
Monitor new shoots closely in spring when infection risk is highest.
Inspect every 7 days during the growing season.
Reapply fungicide after heavy rain if needed.
Apple rust is caused by Gymnosporangium species. Bright orange spots appear on upper leaf surfaces and cause premature leaf drop.
Remove heavily spotted leaves and dispose of them away from the orchard.
Apply a fungicide labeled for rust (e.g., myclobutanil or propiconazole) at petal fall.
Identify and remove nearby juniper or cedar hosts if possible.
Plant resistant apple cultivars in areas with high rust pressure.
Remove or avoid planting junipers within 300 m of apple trees.
Begin protective fungicide sprays at early bud break each spring.
Monitor leaves weekly from bud break through early summer.
Consult local extension service for timing recommendations in your area.
Apple scab is one of the most economically important apple diseases caused by Venturia inaequalis. It produces dark, scaly lesions on leaves and fruit.
Remove and destroy infected leaves and mummified fruit from the ground.
Apply a protectant fungicide (captan, mancozeb) before rain events during primary season.
Switch to a systemic fungicide (myclobutanil) if infection has already occurred.
Rake and compost or bury fallen leaves in autumn to destroy overwintering spores.
Use scab-resistant apple varieties when establishing new orchards.
Maintain a strict spray program from green tip through cover sprays.
Monitor trees weekly during wet spring weather.
Keep records of spray timings to evaluate program effectiveness.
Your plant appears healthy. Keep it thriving with consistent care.
Remove any yellowing leaves as they age.
Monitor weekly for early signs of disease.
We couldn\'t match this to a known condition.
Try a clearer photo of a single leaf in good light.
Avoid wet foliage and keep plants well spaced.
If symptoms persist, consult a local expert.
No additional details available for this prediction.

Frog eye leaf spot is a fungal disease caused by Botryosphaeria obtusa. It appears as circular spots with a light tan/white center and a dark purple border, resembling a frog eye. Heavily infected trees may show early defoliation.

Powdery mildew on apple is caused by Podosphaera leucotricha. It produces a white powdery coating on young leaves and shoots, leading to distorted growth, reduced photosynthesis, and weakened overall tree health.

Apple rust is caused by Gymnosporangium species and appears as bright orange-yellow spots on the upper leaf surface. Rust requires an alternate host (junipers or cedars) to complete its life cycle and can cause significant early leaf drop.

Apple scab, caused by Venturia inaequalis, is one of the most widespread apple diseases. It produces olive-green to dark brown velvety lesions on leaves and fruit, reducing photosynthesis and causing premature defoliation and unmarketable fruit.
The apple leaf appears healthy. No signs of disease detected. Continue good orchard management practices.
Apply fungicides containing captan or thiophanate-methyl. Remove and destroy fallen leaves. Improve canopy airflow through pruning. Reapply fungicide after rain during the growing season.
Apply sulfur-based or systemic fungicides (e.g., myclobutanil). Prune infected shoots. Avoid over-fertilizing with nitrogen. Ensure good airflow around the canopy.
Apply fungicide (myclobutanil or propiconazole) from bud break. Remove nearby juniper or cedar hosts. Remove heavily spotted leaves. Start protective sprays in early spring.

Apply protectant fungicides (captan or mancozeb) before rain events. Switch to systemic fungicides after infection. Rake and remove fallen leaves in autumn. Maintain a regular spray schedule from green tip through harvest.
Continue regular monitoring and maintain good watering, fertilization, and pruning practices to keep the apple tree healthy.
Camera permission is required to take photos
An error occurred. Please try again.
Not an apple leaf. Please upload a clear apple leaf photo.
